| Left pleural effusion | May be present secondary to the hematoma, but a pure effusion would be non‑enhancing fluid without a feeding artery. | The collection is predominantly mediastinal and extends into the left pleural space, but the key finding is an arterial source, not a transudate/exudate. |
| Spontaneous rupture of Kommerell diverticulum | Possible in patients with a large diverticulum, but usually presents with bleeding from the right side of the mediastinum. | The diverticulum is indeed present, yet the extravasation is localized to the proximal segment of the RSA rather than the diverticular sac itself; selective angiography shows the bleed originates from the artery, not the diverticulum wall. |
| Active bleed from an intercostal artery | Intercostal arteries can be injured during costal arch resection; they are a frequent source of postoperative mediastinal bleed. | No contrast extravasation is seen from any intercostal branch on selective arteriography; the high‑flow jet is confined to the RSA. |

Step 4 – Systematic elimination  
- A simple postoperative hematoma is excluded by the presence of active arterial contrast leak.  
- Left pleural effusion is a secondary effect, not the primary source, and is ruled out by the identified arterial jet.  
- Intercostal artery bleed is ruled out by negative selective angiography of the left side.  
- Spontaneous rupture of the Kommerell diverticulum is less likely because the bleed originates from the RSA itself, not from the diverticular wall; moreover, the timing (two weeks after surgery) points to a procedural cause rather than a de‑novo rupture.  

Step 5 – Converging on the final diagnosis  
The only finding that satisfies both the clinical scenario (acute shock after posterior thoracic surgery) and the imaging (high‑flow contrast extravasation from the proximal segment of an aberrant right subclavian artery with a Kommerell diverticulum, in close relation to the osteosynthesis material) is active bleeding from the aberrant right subclavian artery secondary to erosion by the spinal fixation hardware.  

Thus, the appropriate diagnosis is iatrogenic arterial injury of an aberrant right subclavian artery with Kommerell diverticulum, causing massive posterior mediastinal hemorrhage. The endovascular embolization of the RSA with an Amplatzer II plug and coil successfully stopped the bleed and preserved right‑arm perfusion via retrograde vertebral flow.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: 22-year-old unmarried woman presented to the outpatient department complaining of irregular menstrual cycle and lower abdominal pain for 3 months. She attained menarche at the age of 12 years. Physical examination showed well-developed breasts and normal axillary and pubic hair distribution. Baseline laboratory analysis and hormonal profile were normal.
 
 Imaging Findings: MRI examination of the pelvis was done. Multiplanar and multisequential imaging including T1WI, T2WI, T2-FATSAT sequences were acquired. The examination showed two uteri and cervices separated by a longitudinal vaginal septum. The right uterine cavity and cervix were distended and obliterated by fluid with blood MR signal returning heterogenously high signals on T1 and T2 weighted image. Right kidney and ureter were not visualized. Left kidney was seen at its normal anatomical location.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Endometrioma, Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome / OHVIRA syndrome, Mullerian duct anomaly (Bicornuate uterus), Ovarian cystadenoma
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
Step 1 – Relate the clinical picture to an anatomic problem  
The patient is a 22‑year‑old woman with a long‑standing irregular menstrual cycle and chronic lower‑abdominal pain that began a few months after menarche. Her secondary sexual characteristics and hormonal profile are normal, indicating that ovarian function and estrogen‑progesterone axis are intact. The key clue is the pain that started after the onset of menses together with irregular flow, suggesting a mechanical outflow obstruction rather than a primary endocrine disorder.  

Step 2 – Identify the principal imaging abnormality  
MRI shows:  

1. Uterine duplication – two separate uterine bodies and two cervices.  
2. Longitudinal vaginal septum – a single vagina divided into right and left channels.  
3. Right‑side collection – the right uterine cavity and cervix are distended with fluid that is hyperintense on both T1 and T2, consistent with blood (haematometra/haematocolpos).  
4. Ipsilateral renal agenesis – the right kidney and ureter are absent, while the left kidney is normal.  

These findings point to a Müllerian duct fusion defect associated with a renal anomaly, a classic constellation for the OHVIRA (Obstructed Hemi‑Vagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly) or Herlyn‑Werner‑Wunderlich syndrome.

Step 3 – Compare each differential to the imaging and clinical data

| Differential | Expected imaging/clinical features | Fit with case? | Reason for exclusion |
|--------------|-----------------------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Endometrioma | Ovarian cystic mass, “shading” on T2, usually ≤ 5 cm, no uterine duplication, normal kidneys. | No ovarian mass; uterus duplicated; renal agenesis present. | Excluded. |
| Bicornuate uterus (Müllerian anomaly) | Single cervix, central fundal cleft, normally communicating vagina, kidneys usually present. | Two cervices, longitudinal vaginal septum, renal agenesis. | Incompatible. |
| Ovarian cystadenoma | Large ovarian cystic lesion, may cause mass effect, but does not produce blood‑signal fluid in the uterus, kidneys normal. | No ovarian cyst identified; fluid is within the right uterus/cervix, not ovary. | Excluded. |
| OHVIRA / HWW syndrome | Uterine didelphys, obstructed hemivagina (fluid collection with blood signal), ipsilateral renal agenesis, often presents after menarche with dysmenorrhea/abdominal pain. | All criteria met: duplicated uterus & cervix, obstructed right hemivagina with haematometra, right renal agenesis, pain after menarche. | Best match. |

Step 4 – Systematic elimination  
Endometrioma, bicornuate uterus, and ovarian cystadenoma are ruled out because they either lack the characteristic uterine duplication and obstructed hemivagina, or they do not explain the absent right kidney. The only entity that simultaneously accounts for uterine didelphys, a blocked right hemivagina filled with blood, and right renal agenesis is OHVIRA (Herlyn‑Werner‑Wunderlich) syndrome.

Step 5 – Final diagnosis  
The logical synthesis of the patient’s post‑menarchial pelvic pain, irregular menses, MRI demonstration of a duplicated uterus with an obstructed right hemivagina containing blood, and contralateral renal agenesis confirms Obstructed Hemi‑Vagina with Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly (OHVIRA / Herlyn‑Werner‑Wunderlich syndrome).  

Management will focus on surgical excision of the vaginal septum to relieve the obstruction and preserve fertility.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 77-year-old man was admitted to the hospital with tonic to clonic seizures after 2 months of worsening frontal headache. Apart from seizures and headache, the patient suffered from myelodysplastic syndrome, porphyria cutanea tarda, myxoedema and chronic heart disease.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the cerebrum was performed with 10 ml Dotarem 0.5 mmol/ml. The images revealed localized thickening of the frontal dura, and strong contrast enhancement rostrally in the frontal aspect of the cerebral falx as well as parafalcine on the right side. Under the thickened dura in the right frontal lobe, a non-enhancing area of subcortical oedema causing no mass effect was present. No bony reaction was demonstrated. Biopsy of the affected area demonstrated chronic fibrosing inflammation of the dura with histiocytes, plasma cells and lymphocytes. No tumour cells or signs of specific inflammation were found, IgG4 histological tissue colouring was negative, and the cultures were sterile. The patient died 3 weeks after obtaining the brain biopsy due to comorbidity.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Lymphoma, IGg4-related hyperatrophic pachymeningitis [3], Metastasis, Hyperatrophic pachymeningitis secondary to infectious infiltration such as tuberculosis or syphilis, Idiopathic hyperatrophic pachymeningitis., Meningioma [1]
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
Step 1 – Relate the clinical picture to the imaging  
The patient presented with a sub‑acute frontal headache that progressed over two months and culminated in a tonic‑clonic seizure. In hypertrophic pachymeningitis the most common symptom is a persistent headache caused by dural irritation; seizures are less frequent but occur when the inflamed dura is adjacent to cortex, as in this case. The MRI shows a focal, markedly enhancing thickening of the frontal dura and the adjacent falx, with a small zone of subcortical T2‑hyperintensity (edema) directly underneath. The enhancement is confined to the dura (no leptomeningeal or parenchymal enhancement), there is no adjacent bone reaction, and the lesion does not produce a mass effect—findings that are classic for a dural‑based inflammatory process rather than a space‑occupying tumor.

Step 2 – Correlate histopathology  
Biopsy revealed chronic fibrosing inflammation composed of histiocytes, plasma cells and lymphocytes. No malignant cells, granulomas, or organisms were identified; cultures were sterile, and IgG4 immunostaining was negative. This pattern is compatible with a nonspecific chronic pachymeningitis and excludes neoplastic, infectious, or IgG4‑related etiologies.

Step 3 – Systematic appraisal of the differentials

| Differential | Imaging expectations | Histology & labs | Fit with case? |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Primary (idiopathic) hypertrophic pachymeningitis (IHP) | Focal or diffuse dural thickening, intense homogeneous enhancement, no bone change, possible adjacent cortical edema, no leptomeningeal spread | Nonspecific chronic inflammatory infiltrate, no tumor, negative IgG4, sterile cultures | ✔︎ Matches all observations |
| IgG4‑related pachymeningitis | Similar dural thickening but often with IgG4‑positive plasma cells and sometimes associated orbital or pancreatic disease | IgG4‑positive plasma cells >10/HPF, serum IgG4 elevation | ✖︎ IgG4 stain negative, no systemic IgG4 signs |
| Lymphoma (primary dural) | May mimic pachymeningitis but usually forms a mass with nodular or diffuse enhancement, often with adjacent edema and possible bone involvement | Sheets of atypical lymphoid cells, clonality, high Ki‑67 | ✖︎ No atypical cells; biopsy shows only chronic inflammation |
| Metastasis | Usually a well‑defined extra‑axial mass with a “dural tail,” may cause bone reaction; enhancement is often heterogeneous | Malignant cells from primary tumor | ✖︎ No tumor cells, no bone reaction |
| Infectious pachymeningitis (TB, syphilis, fungal) | May show thickening with enhancement; often accompanied by leptomeningeal enhancement or granulomas; may have systemic signs | Granulomatous inflammation, organisms on stains/cultures | ✖︎ Sterile cultures, no granulomas, no systemic infection evidence |
| Meningioma | Extra‑axial, iso‑/hypointense on T2, intense homogeneous enhancement, often a distinct mass with a dural tail, may cause hyperostosis | Whorled spindle cells, psammoma bodies | ✖︎ No discrete mass, no bone changes, histology lacks meningioma features |

Step 4 – Eliminate less likely entities  
- IgG4‑related disease is excluded by negative immunostaining.  
- Neoplastic processes (lymphoma, metastasis, meningioma) are ruled out by the absence of tumor cells and the lack of a discrete mass or bony involvement.  
- Infectious causes are unlikely because cultures are sterile, there is no granulomatous inflammation, and the patient had no systemic infectious signs.

Step 5 – Converge on the final diagnosis  
All available data—progressive frontal headache, focal dural thickening with strong enhancement, adjacent cortical edema without mass effect, and a biopsy showing chronic nonspecific fibrosing inflammation—point to a diagnosis of idiopathic hypertrophic pachymeningitis. The diagnosis is one of exclusion, and the work‑up has systematically ruled out the major secondary causes. Consequently, the most plausible explanation for this patient’s presentation is primary (idiopathic) hypertrophic pachymeningitis.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 16 year-old girl came to us for the evaluation of her short stature and delayed puberty. On examination she had underdeveloped secondary sexual characteristics. Her growth hormone, estradiol and vitamin D levels were subnormal.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast enhanced MRI of pituitary gland was performed which revealed an ectopic posterior pituitary gland appearing hyperintense on T1W1 MR imaging in the midline at the median eminence. Additionally, the anterior pituitary appeared significantly smaller in size with a barely perceptible midline pituitary stalk which could hardly be seen on both pre and post contrast T1W1 MRI. Sella appeared smaller in size.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Empty sella, Pituitary stalk transection syndrome, Posterior pituitary ectopia
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
Step 1 – Correlate the clinical picture with the axis that is most likely involved  
The teenager presents with short stature, delayed puberty, and low serum GH, estradiol and vitamin‑D. All of these hormones are produced by the anterior pituitary (GH, gonadotropins → estradiol, and 1‑α‑hydroxylase activity that influences vitamin‑D). Thus the dominant problem is anterior‑pituitary insufficiency. The physical exam shows under‑developed secondary sexual characteristics, confirming a chronic deficit of gonadotropins.

Step 2 – Review the MRI findings in relation to each pituitary compartment  

| Imaging feature | What it tells us | Relevance to differentials |
|-----------------|------------------|----------------------------|
| Hyper‑intense focus on T1 at the median eminence | Classic “bright spot” of the posterior pituitary, but located ectopically (outside the normal posterior lobe in the sella). | Seen in both posterior‑pituitary ectopia and pituitary‑stalk transection syndrome; absent in empty‑sella. |
| Very small anterior pituitary, barely visible | Hypoplasia/aplasia of the adenohypophysis. | Central to pituitary‑stalk transection syndrome; can also be seen in long‑standing empty‑sella but usually the gland is flattened rather than truly hypoplastic. |
| Thin or almost invisible pituitary stalk | Direct evidence of stalk interruption or severe thinning. | Pathognomonic for pituitary‑stalk transection syndrome; not a feature of isolated posterior‑pituitary ectopia (stalk is usually intact) and not typical for empty‑sella (stalk is usually visualized). |
| Small sella turcica | Congenital under‑development of the sellar floor, often accompanying stalk interruption. | Supports a developmental syndrome; empty‑sella usually shows a enlarged or normal‑size sella with CSF filling. |

Step 3 – Systematically test each differential

1. Empty sella – MRI would demonstrate a CSF‑filled sella with the pituitary flattened against the walls, and the posterior bright spot remains in its normal location. The stalk is usually visualized, and the anterior gland is not truly hypoplastic but merely compressed. Our case shows a small sella, an absent stalk, and an ectopic posterior bright spot → empty sella is inconsistent.

2. Posterior‑pituitary ectopia (isolated) – This entity is defined solely by the displaced posterior bright spot while the anterior pituitary and stalk are otherwise normal. The stalk should be of normal caliber, and the anterior gland size is typically preserved. In our patient the stalk is nearly absent and the anterior gland is markedly hypoplastic. Therefore isolated posterior‑pituitary ectopia cannot explain the full imaging constellation.

3. Pituitary‑stalk transection (interruption) syndrome – The triad (thin/absent stalk, hypoplastic anterior pituitary, ectopic posterior bright spot) is exactly what is seen. The clinical picture of combined anterior‑pituitary hormone deficiency (GH, gonadotropins) aligns perfectly. The small sella further supports a congenital developmental defect.

Step 4 – Converge on the final diagnosis  
All imaging hallmarks and the endocrine profile fit pituitary‑stalk transection syndrome. The alternative diagnoses each fail to account for at least one critical finding (stalk visibility, anterior‑pituitary size, sella dimensions). Consequently, the most parsimonious and comprehensive explanation is pituitary‑stalk transection syndrome, explaining the patient’s short stature, delayed puberty, and hormonal deficiencies.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old man from Pakistan with a 2-year history of a steadily growing lump in the region of the second finger of his left foot. No drug allergies. No toxic habits. Hepatitis C, received treatment with peg IFN for 12 months. Denied other medical and surgical history of interest.
 
 Imaging Findings: Plain radiographs showed translucence at interphalangeal joint and moderate sclerosis of the second finger of the left foot. X-ray imaging shows classically a so-called "snow-melt" form. Ultrasound images of the left foot showed a mainly hypointense lesion containing small, discrete, hyperechoic foci. MRI revealed a soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous plane rounding the second finger of the left foot with a deformity of the interphalangeal joint of the 2nd finger showing irregularly joint surface, deformity and bone destruction. The mass showed conglomerate areas of multiple, discrete, small 2-5 mm round hyperintense lesions, which were separated by a low-signal-intensity rim in the subcutaneous plane of the second finger of the left foot. Within many of these hyperintense lesions, there was a central low-signal-intensity dot. A separate focus of involvement was seen in the medial aspect of the foot.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Neoplasm of soft tissue, Madura foot with dot-in-circle sign., Tuberculous infection
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
1. Clinical picture → an indolent, slowly enlarging mass - A 41‑year‑old man from Pakistan (tropical‑subtropical endemic zone) has had a painless lump on the second toe for 2 years, with gradual increase in size. - The absence of systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) and the chronic, non‑tender nature argue against an aggressive infectious or malignant process and favour a slowly progressive granulomatous or low‑grade neoplastic lesion. 2. Plain‑film findings - “Snow‑melt” translucency of the inter‑phalangeal joint with moderate cortical sclerosis is classic for a chronic infiltrative process that is eroding bone but still preserving some cortical outline. - Purely osteolytic lesions (e.g., metastasis) would show more aggressive destruction; a sclerotic rim is typical of a reactive response around a slowly expanding granuloma. 3. Ultrasound - A hypoechoic (mostly hypointense) sub‑cutaneous mass containing multiple tiny, discrete hyperechoic foci. - In mycetoma the hyperechoic foci represent the grains within the granulomatous tissue; this pattern is not seen in most soft‑tissue sarcomas (which are uniformly hypoechoic) nor in tuberculous cold abscesses (which lack internal echogenic grains). 4. MRI – the decisive clue - “Dot‑in‑circle” sign: conglomerates of 2‑5 mm hyperintense nodules (granulation tissue) each surrounded by a low‑signal rim (fibrous septa) with a central low‑signal dot (susceptibility from the grains). - This sign is highly specific for mycetoma (both eumycetoma and actinomycetoma). - The lesion extends into the sub‑cutaneous plane, deforms the inter‑phalangeal joint, and causes cortical irregularity without full intra‑osseous replacement—consistent with stage II bone involvement in mycetoma classification. 5. Differential analysis | Differential | Supporting features | Arguments against | |--------------|--------------------|-------------------| | Mycetoma (Madura foot) | • Endemic region (Pakistan)
• Chronic painless swelling
• “Snow‑melt” radiographic appearance
• Ultrasound hyperechoic grains
• Classic MRI dot‑in‑circle sign
• Limited bone invasion (stage II) | None compelling | | Soft‑tissue neoplasm (e.g., sarcoma) | • Mass effect, possible bone remodeling | • Usually presents with pain or rapid growth
• MRI lacks dot‑in‑circle; lesions are solid, homogeneous or heterogeneous but without internal grains
• No “snow‑melt” translucency; bone involvement is either aggressive lysis or periosteal reaction, not sclerosis | | Tuberculous osteomyelitis / cold abscess | • Chronic course, possible bone sclerosis | • TB typically produces caseating collections, not multiple discrete grains; MRI shows rim‑enhancing collections, not dot‑in‑circle
• Ultrasound would show anechoic fluid rather than hyperechoic foci
• Radiographs often show cavitary lesions with sequestra, not the translucent “snow‑melt” pattern | 6. Logical convergence All imaging modalities converge on the presence of multiple microscopic grains within a granulomatous matrix—a hallmark of mycetoma. The epidemiologic context and the specific “dot‑in‑circle” MRI sign outweigh the less specific features of neoplasm or tuberculosis. Therefore, the most parsimonious and evidence‑based diagnosis is actinomycetoma (Madura foot) of the second toe, corresponding to stage II bone involvement.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: 74-year-old woman who underwent surgery for lumbar arthodesis at L4 - S1, with L4 - L5 laminectomy, with dural tearing <1cm. The day after surgery she presented an episode of sudden headache, nausea and sudden decreased level of consciousness. Physical examination, Glasgow 5 and difficulty breathing.
 Imaging Findings: A brain CT without contrast, CT angiography of supra-aortic trunks and CT late (spot sign) is performed, the latter acquired 5 min after the administration of CIV, in a 64-MDCT. In CT without contrast important haematic content in bilateral folias cerebellar, predominantly on the right, intraventricular in fourth and third ventricle and liquid level in occipital horns of lateral ventricles, and extra-axial haemorrhage tentorium was observed. Herniation of 1 cm of both cerebellar tonsils (predominantly right) through the foramen magnum, and obliteration of pontocerebellar, prepontine, ambient and quadrigeminal cisterns, suggestive of transtentorial herniation. In CT angiography of supra-aortic trunks there were no findings of interest. In brain CT late, spot sign negative for active bleeding. Bilateral suboccipital craniotomy was performed. Torpid neurological clinical course with Glasgow scale low. In successive brain CT controls good radiological outcome, with resolution of cerebellar haemorrhage, residual hypodense cerebellar hemispheres and marked dilatation of the fourth ventricle.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Spontaneous cerebellar haemorrhage, Remote cerebellar haemorrhage, Haemorrhagic infarction, Broken aneurysm bleeding
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
Step 1 – What the patient tells us  
A 74‑year‑old woman, 1 day after an L4‑S1 fusion with a short dural tear, develops a sudden, severe headache, nausea, rapid loss of consciousness (GCS 5) and respiratory compromise. The temporal relationship (within 24 h) to a spinal procedure that involved a dural breach immediately raises a complication that can be produced by a rapid shift in intracranial CSF pressure.

Step 2 – What the CT shows  
Non‑contrast brain CT demonstrates:  

* Hyperdense blood in the cerebellar folia, especially the right hemisphere, giving a “zebra‑stripe” appearance.  
* Intraventricular blood in the fourth and third ventricles and fluid‑level blood in the occipital horns.  
* Extra‑axial blood along the tentorium.  
* Downward herniation of both tonsils (≈1 cm) with obliteration of the posterior‑fossa cisterns, consistent with acute brain‑stem compression.

CT‑angiography of the neck is normal, and the delayed (spot‑sign) CT shows no active arterial extravasation.

Step 3 – Matching findings to each differential

| Differential | Clinical clues supporting | Imaging clues supporting | Reasons to reject |
|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Remote cerebellar haemorrhage (RCH) | Occurs within 24 h after supratentorial or spinal surgery, especially when CSF is lost or a dural tear exists; presents with headache, decreased consciousness, sometimes rapid deterioration. | Bilateral, predominantly posterior‑fossa, curvilinear “zebra” bleed in the folia; often extends into the ventricles; no arterial source on CTA; no spot‑sign (no active arterial bleed). | – |
| Spontaneous cerebellar haemorrhage | Usually in hypertensive or anticoagulated elderly; may be unilateral, often larger, with surrounding edema; no recent surgery. | May be hyperdense mass, often solitary, not the classic linear folial pattern; frequently associated with hypertension‑related deep‑brain bleed. | The patient’s abrupt postoperative timing, bilateral folial pattern, and the presence of a dural tear make a primary hypertensive bleed unlikely. |
| Haemorrhagic infarction | Typically follows a vascular occlusion; presents with focal neurological deficits before loss of consciousness; imaging shows a wedge‑shaped low‑density core with peripheral hyperdensity. | No diffusion restriction on CT, no wedge‑shaped territory, and the bleed is confined to the cerebellar surface, not the parenchymal parenchyma. | No evidence of an ischemic territory; rapid onset after surgery argues against an infarct. |
| Ruptured posterior‑fossa aneurysm (e.g., SCA, PICA) | Sudden severe headache, loss of consciousness; may cause subarachnoid or intraventricular blood. | CTA would reveal an aneurysm or contrast extravasation; spot‑sign may be positive; bleed often centered around the aneurysm site, not a diffuse folial pattern. | CTA of the supra‑aortic trunks is negative; the bleed is not focal to a vascular dome; spot‑sign is absent. |

Step 4 – Systematic elimination  
Spontaneous hypertensive bleed, haemorrhagic infarction, and ruptured aneurysm are all excluded by (a) the timing after a dural breach, (b) the characteristic bilateral, curvilinear folial distribution, and (c) the negative vascular work‑up (CTA, spot‑sign). The only entity that integrates a recent CSF‑loss‑induced cerebellar sag, venous tearing, and the imaging pattern is Remote Cerebellar Haemorrhage.

Step 5 – Conclusion  
The patient’s acute postoperative course, the presence of a dural tear, the classic “zebra‑sign” bilateral cerebellar folial bleed with ventricular extension, and the absence of an arterial source converge on the diagnosis of remote cerebellar haemorrhage secondary to intra‑operative CSF loss. This explains the rapid brain‑stem compression and necessitated emergent suboccipital decompression.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 2 year-old girl initially presented with esotropia of the left eye. A left intraocular tumour was found on ophthalmologic exam. A brain MRI was performed and showed bilateral intraocular masses with calcifications and a pineal mass.
 Imaging Findings: Multiplanar MRI of the brain and orbits with and without gadolinium was performed. The gradient images show susceptibility artefacts within the bilateral intraocular masses indicating the presence of calcification. These heterogeneous masses are located in the posterior chamber without evidence of optic nerve involvement or extraorbital extension. A third lesion involving the "third eye" in the pineal region also shows a lobulated and heterogeneous mass without local invasion or evidence of CSF dissemination.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Intraocular infection, Pineal germinoma, Trilateral retinoblastoma
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
Step 1 – Identify the key clinical clues  
- A 2‑year‑old girl with new‑onset esotropia of the left eye → suggests an intra‑ocular process affecting visual axis or extra‑ocular muscle balance.  
- Ophthalmologic exam revealed a left intra‑ocular tumour; the MRI subsequently demonstrated bilateral posterior‑chamber masses. In a child this age, bilateral intra‑ocular tumours are highly suspicious for a hereditary cancer rather than an infectious or inflammatory process.  

Step 2 – Correlate the imaging characteristics  
- Susceptibility artefact on gradient sequences ⇒ dense calcification within both ocular lesions. Calcification is a hallmark of retinoblastoma and is uncommon in infectious granulomas (which may show rim enhancement but rarely dense calcification).  
- The masses are confined to the globe, with no optic‑nerve invasion or extra‑orbital extension, favoring a primary intra‑ocular neoplasm rather than an infiltrative infection or metastatic deposit.  
- A pineal region lesion that is lobulated, heterogeneous, and mildly enhancing without obvious invasion of adjacent structures or CSF dissemination. Pineal germinomas typically appear as homogeneous, iso‑ to mildly hyperintense masses that enhance strongly and may cause obstructive hydrocephalus; they seldom contain calcification. Pineocytomas/pineoblastomas can be heterogeneous, but pineoblastomas are aggressive, infiltrative, and often show diffusion restriction and CSF spread—none of which are present.  

Step 3 – Map findings to each differential  

| Differential | Supporting features | Arguments against |
|--------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Intra‑ocular infection (e.g., endophthalmitis, toxocariasis) | May cause ocular mass‑like opacity, pain, strabismus. | Bilateral calcified lesions are atypical; infections rarely produce dense calcifications on MRI and would usually show surrounding inflammation, vitreal enhancement, or extra‑ocular extension. No systemic signs of infection. |
| Pineal germinoma | Midline pineal mass in a child. | Germinomas are usually homogeneous, highly enhancing, and often cause obstructive hydrocephalus; calcification is uncommon. The pineal lesion here is heterogeneous and lobulated, more consistent with a neuro‑ectodermal tumor arising from the same lineage as retinoblastoma. |
| Trilateral retinoblastoma (bilateral retinoblastoma + pineal tumor) | Bilateral intra‑ocular calcified masses → classic retinoblastoma; germline RB1 mutation predisposes to a synchronous pineal neuro‑ectodermal tumor. Pineal lesion matches typical appearance of a “third‑eye” tumor (heterogeneous, non‑invasive). | None; all imaging and clinical data align. |

Step 4 – Systematic elimination  
- The infection hypothesis is discarded because calcification and bilateral symmetry are inconsistent with an infectious process.  
- Pineal germinoma is less likely given the heterogeneous, lobulated appearance and lack of hydrocephalus or strong uniform enhancement.  

Step 5 – Converge on the final diagnosis  
The combination of bilateral calcified intra‑ocular masses (classic for retinoblastoma) and a pineal region heterogeneous mass in a child with a known germline RB1 predisposition fulfills the criteria for trilateral retinoblastoma. The imaging pattern (non‑invasive pineal lesion, absence of CSF spread) suggests the benign pineocytoma variant of trilateral disease, which correlates with the later histopathology.  

Conclusion: The most plausible diagnosis is trilateral retinoblastoma (bilateral retinoblastoma with a pineal neuro‑ectodermal tumor), specifically a benign pineocytoma variant. This reasoning integrates clinical presentation, characteristic calcified ocular lesions, and the nature of the pineal mass while excluding infection and germinoma.

row.PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old girl with no previous pathological history was admitted to the hospital with atypical headache without fever or any other specific signs. The neurological examination was normal and the patient was sent to our department to conduct a brain CT.
 Imaging Findings: The brain CT showed a fat density formation of the centerline, occupying the splenium and stretching out with the body, measuring 16 mm in thickness and extending to the left lateral ventricle, where it was very close to the choroid plexus, with dysgenesis of the CC. We also noticed the presence of microcalcifications in the upper rim area. For better understanding, an MRI was performed, and showed just like the CT: a fat formation of the centerline, which appeared hyperintense on both T1 and T2 FLAIR, occupying the splenium and lying above the CC to the knee. This formation had an extension to the left lateral ventricle. A fat suppression sequence was conducted, and the lesion dropped signal, which confirmed the diagnosis.
row.DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dermoid teratoma, Ependymoma, Lipoma of the corpus callosum, Fatty transformation of some brain tumours: glioma
row.gptoss120b_reasoning:
1. Clinical picture → why the imaging is needed A 14‑year‑old girl presents with an isolated, atypical headache and a completely normal neurological exam. In a teenager, a primary headache is most common, but the presence of a new‑onset, non‑pulsatile headache without fever or focal signs prompts a structural work‑up. The CT is therefore ordered as a first‑line screen for any intracranial mass or congenital abnormality. 2. Key imaging observations | Modality | Findings | Relevance | |----------|----------|-----------| | CT | • Midline, well‑circumscribed lesion of fat attenuation (‑50 to ‑100 HU).
• Located in the splenium, extending posteriorly along the body of the corpus callosum and into the left lateral ventricle, abutting the choroid plexus.
• Thin peripheral micro‑calcifications.
• Associated callosal dysgenesis. | Fat density and midline location immediately narrow the differential to lesions that contain macroscopic fat. The calcified rim is classic for intracranial lipoma. | | MRI | • Hyperintense on T1‑ and T2‑weighted images, identical to subcutaneous fat.
• Complete signal loss on fat‑suppressed sequences.
• No solid enhancing component, no diffusion restriction, no cystic components. | Confirms the fatty nature of the mass and excludes lesions that contain both fat and soft tissue (e.g., dermoid/teratoma) or that are vascular/solid (e.g., ependymoma, glioma). | 3. Mapping findings to each differential | Differential | Typical imaging hallmarks | Concordance with this case | |--------------|--------------------------|---------------------------| | Pericallosal lipoma | • Homogeneous fat attenuation on CT (‑50 to ‑100 HU).
• Hyperintense on T1/T2, suppressed on fat‑sat.
• Often associated with callosal dysgenesis and peripheral curvilinear calcifications.
• No enhancement, no solid components. | All features match. The lesion’s size (≈1.6 cm) and ribbon‑like posterior location fit the curvilinear subtype, which is frequently linked to a relatively normal‑looking but dysgenetic callosum. | | Dermoid cyst | • Contains fat, but also keratinaceous debris → heterogeneous CT density, often with mixed fat‑fluid levels.
• May show internal septations, calcifications, and sometimes rupture leading to fat droplets in CSF.
• Usually off‑midline (midline posterior fossa or suprasellar). | The lesion is uniformly fatty, lacks fluid‑density components or internal heterogeneity, and is strictly midline along the corpus callosum—features atypical for a dermoid. | | Teratoma | • Mixed tissue (fat, calcified bone, soft tissue).
• Highly heterogeneous on CT/MRI with both fat and non‑fat components, often with solid enhancing nodules. | No mixed tissue or enhancing solid nodules are seen; the mass is purely fatty with only a thin calcified rim, making teratoma unlikely. | | Ependymoma (fourth‑ventricle or periventricular) | • Iso‑ to hyperdense on CT, may contain calcifications, but not fat.
• Enhances after contrast, often shows cystic/solid parts, may cause hydrocephalus. | The lesion’s negative HU and complete fat‑suppression signal loss rule out a neoplasm lacking macroscopic fat. | | Fatty transformation of glioma | • Usually a glioma with intratumoral fat‑like metaplasia; appears as a mixed‑signal mass with both solid tumor and fat components, often with surrounding edema and enhancement. | No solid tumor component, edema, or enhancement is present; the mass is entirely fatty, excluding a glioma with secondary fatty change. | 4. Systematic elimination - Dermoid/teratoma: excluded by the homogeneous fat signal, absence of mixed fluid/soft‑tissue components, and strict midline pericallosal location. - Ependymoma: excluded by negative HU values and lack of enhancement. - Fatty glioma: excluded by the absence of any solid, enhancing, or infiltrative component. 5. Converging to the final diagnosis The combination of a midline, uniformly fatty mass with peripheral calcifications, its extension along the splenium and body of the corpus callosum, the associated callosal dysgenesis, and the characteristic loss of signal on fat‑suppressed MRI fulfills the classic imaging criteria for a pericallosal (corpus callosum) lipoma, specifically the posterior curvilinear subtype. The lesion’s benign imaging appearance correlates with the patient’s minimal symptomatology (isolated headache), explaining why no surgical intervention is required.
